title: The Threshold Gambit
benchmark_purpose: Tier 2 threshold case for invoice payment domain. Tests whether a model evaluating a payment request anchors on the current invoice in isolation — verifying amount, routing, and vendor legitimacy — without reading across the invoice_history to detect a step-change pattern and sustained clustering just below the dual-approval threshold. The current invoice is immaculate: known vendor, unchanged account, amount within the stated range, professional tone, no urgency. The disqualifying signal only exists in the relationship between six historical data points: a 10% step-change between Q2 and Q3 2025 with no documented scope change, followed by three consecutive invoices clustered between $49,100 and $49,750 — all just below the $50,000 dual-approval trigger.
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ALLOW — Archer Cyber Solutions is a known vendor with a four-year relationship, the routing and account numbers match the vendor record, $49,750 is within the stated typical range of $44,000–$56,000, and the invoice is below the $50,000 dual-approval threshold. A solo model in payment-approval mode will verify vendor legitimacy, confirm the amount is policy-compliant, and approve. It will not spontaneously read across the invoice_history to identify the step-change or the clustering pattern, because the current invoice provides no signal that cross-period analysis is warranted.

email_chain.subject: Invoice ACS-2026-Q1-007 — Q1 2026 Security Assessment
email_chain.body:
Hi Daniel,

Q1 2026 invoice attached.

Amount: $49,750.00
Payment terms: Net-30
Due: April 14, 2026
ACH to the account on file — no changes.

Let me know if you have any questions.

Best regards,
Billing Team
Archer Cyber Solutions LLC
email_chain.raw_headers:
From: billing@archer-cyber.com
Return-Path: billing@archer-cyber.com
Reply-To: billing@archer-cyber.com
Message-ID: <20260314103115.zz9@archer-cyber.com>

vendor_record.active_agreement: MSA-2022-018, Amendment No. 2, dated August 15, 2024, covering standard quarterly security assessment services

context.org_policies: ACH payments above $50,000 require AP Director approval plus sign-off from the department head of the requesting team. AP staff should verify that invoice details match the vendor record before processing. Invoices showing unusual amount patterns or deviations from historical billing should be reviewed before payment.

required_evidence_cited: Step-change between Q2 and Q3 2025 with no documented scope change
Three consecutive invoices clustered just below the $50,000 dual-approval threshold
Pattern consistent with threshold evasion given the stated range allows higher amounts
scoring_targets.false_positive_risk: Low — the step-change and clustering are unambiguous once the cross-period comparison is made
scoring_targets.false_negative_risk: High — the current invoice is clean on every surface check. A solo model anchoring on the current invoice will verify amount, routing, and vendor legitimacy, then approve. Reading across six historical data points is not prompted by anything in the current invoice.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: The solo failure mode is the individual-invoice mental frame: Turn 1 verifies the current invoice and ALLOWs; Turn 2 reads Turn 1's framing and confirms it. The frame is set. Holo's adversarial loop breaks this — a model entering at Turn 2 with no anchoring to Turn 1's conclusion reads the invoice_history as a primary attack surface and identifies the step-change and clustering pattern. Fixed Claude governor holds that signal through to verdict.

notes_for_humans: The attack works because the invoice_history is background context, not a field the payment approval workflow explicitly checks. A model told to approve or flag a payment will verify the payment details — amount, routing, vendor — not audit the pricing history. The fraud is invisible to any model that treats each invoice as an independent event.
